name: Stardust Valley
description: Stardust Valley - A warm coastal valley world of cozy towns, supernatural beings living ordinary lives, and soft magic. VchiBan-inspired.

1.content: Stardust Valley is a warm coastal valley world where supernatural beings and humans live side by side without conflict or curiosity. Fox-eared shopkeepers sell hand-dyed fabrics next to human bakers. Spirits tend gardens alongside elderly couples. No one questions this arrangement because there is nothing to question — it has always been this way. The valley runs from forested hills down to a rocky coastline dotted with fishing villages and tide pools that glow faintly after dark. The climate is mild year-round, with gentle rains in spring and golden light that lasts well into evening during summer. Stardust Valley asks nothing of its visitors except that they be kind.

2.content: Life in Stardust Valley follows a rhythm tied to seasons and markets rather than clocks. Mornings begin with bread baking and tea. Afternoons are for work — tending shops, fishing, crafting, foraging. Evenings are communal: markets open at dusk, lanterns are lit by hand, and people gather to eat, trade stories, and listen to music. There is no urgency. Deadlines are suggestions. Appointments are approximate. This is not laziness — it is a culture that has decided rushing serves no one. Visitors from faster worlds often find the first day frustrating and the third day healing.

3.content: Supernatural beings in Stardust Valley are not rare, exotic, or hidden. They are neighbors. A kitsune runs the fabric shop on Market Street. A river spirit maintains the oldest bridge in town. A family of tanuki operates a bathhouse that has been open for six generations. These beings have animal features — fox ears, antlers, scales, feathered arms — that mark them as non-human, but no one treats this as remarkable. Supernatural residents age differently, see slightly more of the world than humans do, and occasionally perform small magic as naturally as breathing. The coexistence is not a treaty or an agreement. It is simply how things are.

4.content: Stardust Valley experiences four distinct seasons, each with its own character. Spring brings soft rains and wildflower blooms that carpet the hillsides in pale purple and gold. Summer is warm and long, with golden light that stretches evenings into something that feels like a held breath. Autumn turns the valley amber and brings the harvest festivals — the busiest and most joyful time of year. Winter is mild but present, with frost on morning grass and woodsmoke from every chimney. Snow is rare and treated as an event when it arrives. The weather here feels personal, as though the valley itself has preferences about what kind of day it wants to have.

5.content: The Lantern Market opens every evening at dusk in the town square of Hearthstone, the valley's largest settlement. Paper lanterns are hung from lines strung between buildings, casting warm light over rows of wooden stalls. Vendors sell handmade goods — dyed fabrics, carved wooden figures, herbal remedies, fresh pastries, and trinkets that may or may not be mildly enchanted. The market is as much a social event as a commercial one. People come to browse, eat, gossip, and listen to whoever has brought an instrument. Prices are flexible and haggling is friendly. The market closes when the last vendor decides they are tired, which varies.

6.content: Hearthstone is the largest settlement in Stardust Valley, built where the river meets the coast. Cobblestone streets wind between buildings of warm stone and dark timber. Most structures are two or three stories, with flower boxes in every window and cats on every roof. The town square hosts the Lantern Market each evening. Key landmarks include the Foxglove Tea House, the community bathhouse, and the old stone bridge maintained by the river spirit who has lived beneath it longer than anyone can verify. Hearthstone has no walls, no gates, and no guards — the valley has never needed them.

7.content: A string of small fishing villages lines the rocky coast south of Hearthstone. Each village is no more than a dozen buildings — homes, a drying shed, a boat repair lean-to. The fisherfolk here are a mix of human and supernatural, working side by side on boats that have been patched so many times the original wood is a matter of debate. The tide pools between villages glow faintly at night with bioluminescent life that no one has formally studied. Children collect shells and small glowing creatures in jars. The coastal path connecting the villages is one of the most walked routes in the valley, worn smooth by generations of feet.

8.content: The Foxglove Tea House sits on the corner of the town square in Hearthstone, run by a soft-spoken kitsune named Sora who has operated it for longer than most residents have been alive. The interior is warm wood and cushioned seating, with shelves of dried herbs and tea blends lining every wall. Sora blends teas to suit the person rather than the order — regulars have learned to trust whatever appears in their cup. The tea house serves as an informal gathering place where news is shared, arguments are settled over refills, and visitors are gently introduced to the valley's pace of life.

9.content: Moonveil Shrine sits at the top of the forested hill overlooking Hearthstone. It is a simple wooden structure — open-sided, with a curved roof and a stone basin for offerings. No one is certain who built it or what it is dedicated to. The shrine keepers maintain it out of respect for tradition rather than any specific deity. Visitors leave small offerings — flowers, coins, written wishes folded into paper shapes — and the offerings are always gone by morning, though no one has seen them removed. The view from the shrine at sunset is considered the most beautiful in the valley. Many important conversations happen here.

10.content: The Whispering Woods cover the hills along the valley's northern edge. The trees are old-growth — thick trunks, high canopies, moss on everything. The forest floor is soft with fallen leaves and scattered with mushrooms that glow faintly in the shade. The woods are called whispering because the wind through the canopy sounds like murmured conversation just below the threshold of comprehension. Foragers gather herbs, mushrooms, and berries here. Deeper in, the trees grow closer together and the paths become less defined. Locals advise visitors to stay on known trails, not because the woods are dangerous but because getting lost here takes longer to fix than expected.

11.content: Natural hot springs emerge from the hillside above Hearthstone, feeding a series of stone pools that have been used for communal bathing for generations. The main bathhouse is operated by a tanuki family who keep the pools clean, the water temperature comfortable, and the atmosphere peaceful. Bathing is a social activity — conversations happen here that happen nowhere else, with a frankness enabled by the informality of the setting. The mineral water is said to have mild restorative properties, though whether this is the minerals or simply the act of sitting still in warm water for an hour is a matter of friendly debate.

12.content: Driftwood Cove is a sheltered beach at the southern end of the coastal path, accessible through a narrow gap in the sea cliffs. The beach is covered in smooth driftwood that has been shaped by the ocean into forms that occasionally look intentional — a chair, a frame, a figure reaching. Local artists collect driftwood here for carving and construction. At low tide, the cove reveals rock formations and pools that contain creatures found nowhere else along the coast. It is a popular spot for solitude, for thinking, and for the kind of conversation that requires being away from everyone else.

13.content: Bramble Row is a narrow street in Hearthstone where most of the valley's artisans keep their workshops. The name comes from the flowering brambles that grow along every fence and wall, blooming in white and pink regardless of season. Workshops here produce pottery, woven textiles, carved wood, herbal remedies, and musical instruments. Most artisans learned their craft from a parent or mentor and will teach it to whoever asks with genuine interest. The street smells of sawdust, dye, and whatever the herbalist is drying that week. Bramble Row is quieter during the day when people are working and lively in the hour before the Lantern Market opens.

14.content: The Shrine Keepers are a small group of volunteers who maintain Moonveil Shrine and the smaller roadside shrines scattered throughout the valley. They sweep, replace worn wood, ensure the offering basin is clean, and light incense at dawn and dusk. The role is not religious in any formal sense — there is no doctrine, no prayer, no hierarchy. Shrine Keepers simply believe that some places deserve tending. Membership is open to anyone willing to show up consistently. The current head keeper is Maeve, an elderly human woman who has tended the hilltop shrine for forty years and answers questions about its purpose with a shrug and a smile.

15.content: The Fishing Cooperative is an informal organization of the coastal village fisherfolk who share boats, equipment, and catches. Membership requires only that you fish and share honestly. The cooperative ensures that no family goes without food during poor seasons and that boats are maintained collectively rather than individually. Meetings happen on the docks at dawn before boats go out and consist mostly of weather discussion, route planning, and good-natured arguments about bait. The cooperative includes both human and supernatural members, some of whom have physical advantages in the water that no one comments on because the fish don't care who catches them.

16.content: The Market Vendors Guild is a loose association of Lantern Market merchants who coordinate stall placement, resolve disputes, and organize seasonal market events. The guild has no formal leadership — decisions are made by whoever shows up to the monthly meeting at the Foxglove Tea House. Membership dues are a single seasonal dish brought to share. The guild's primary function is ensuring fairness: no vendor undercuts another deliberately, new vendors are given good stall placement for their first season, and anyone caught selling something genuinely harmful is asked to leave once and told to leave twice.

17.content: The Herbalists Circle is a collective of healers, foragers, and remedy-makers who gather weekly to share knowledge, trade ingredients, and discuss what the forest is producing this season. Members include humans who learned from grandparents and supernatural beings whose understanding of plants is instinctive and older than language. The circle maintains a shared garden behind the Bramble Row workshops where rare and useful plants are cultivated. Their remedies are the valley's primary medicine — teas for fever, poultices for wounds, tinctures for sleeplessness. The circle does not charge for remedies given to those in need. For everyone else, prices are fair and negotiable.

18.content: The Storytelling Circle meets on the last evening of each month in the town square after the Lantern Market closes. Anyone may tell a story. The only rule is that you must tell it — not read it. Stories range from local history to family legends to supernatural encounters to pure invention. The circle is how the valley preserves its history, since written records are sparse and considered less reliable than a well-told tale. Supernatural members of the circle sometimes tell stories that span centuries, delivered with the casual precision of someone describing what they had for breakfast. The best storytellers are not the loudest but the ones who make you forget you are listening.

19.content: The Valley Watch is a volunteer patrol that walks the paths between villages, checks on isolated homesteads, and helps travelers who have gotten lost in the Whispering Woods. They are not guards or soldiers — the valley has no crime that requires either. The Watch exists because the paths can be long, the weather can turn, and sometimes an elderly resident hasn't been seen in a few days and someone should check. Members carry lanterns, basic first aid supplies, and enough food to share. The Watch is coordinated by Finn, a quiet young man who seems to know every trail in the valley by feel and who is always the first to notice when someone is missing.

20.content: Shopkeepers in Stardust Valley run their businesses with warmth and minimal urgency. A typical shopkeeper knows every regular customer by name, remembers their preferences, and will set aside items they think someone might want before being asked. Shops open when the owner wakes up and close when they decide the day is done. Haggling is friendly and often ends with both parties feeling they got the better deal. Supernatural shopkeepers may have been running their establishments for decades or centuries, accumulating knowledge of their craft that borders on the uncanny. All shopkeepers will feed you if you look hungry, regardless of whether you buy anything.

21.content: Fisherfolk in Stardust Valley are practical, weather-aware, and generous with their catch. They rise before dawn, know the tides by instinct, and can read the sky with more accuracy than any instrument. Most learned to fish from a parent or grandparent and speak about the ocean with a respect that borders on reverence. Fishing stories are a recognized art form — exaggeration is expected and appreciated. Supernatural fisherfolk may have gills, webbed fingers, or an ability to hold their breath far longer than any human, and none of this is remarked upon because the boats need crewing regardless of who has fins.

22.content: Herbalists in Stardust Valley are part healer, part forager, part counselor. They know which plants grow where, which mushrooms are safe, and which combinations of dried leaves will cure a headache or help someone sleep. Most herbalists work from small workshops on Bramble Row or from kitchen tables in their homes. They listen as much as they prescribe — a visit to the herbalist often involves a cup of tea and a conversation about what is actually wrong before any remedy is discussed. Supernatural herbalists sometimes communicate with plants directly, receiving impressions of health or distress that guide their harvesting.

23.content: Supernatural residents of Stardust Valley are beings with animal features, extended lifespans, and casual access to small magic. A kitsune might light a lantern by breathing on it. A river spirit might know when rain is coming three days in advance. A tanuki might shift their appearance slightly when they think no one is looking. These abilities are used practically — to help with work, to ease daily life, to entertain children. Supernatural residents do not consider themselves special or separate. They have jobs, families, preferences, and bad days like everyone else. The main difference is temporal — they remember things from longer ago and sometimes struggle with the pace at which human friends age.

24.content: Wandering musicians pass through Stardust Valley regularly, drawn by the Lantern Market's welcoming atmosphere and the valley's appreciation for live performance. They play at the market, at the tea house, at the hot springs, and sometimes simply on a street corner for whoever stops to listen. Instruments range from string instruments to flutes to drums to things that have no name but produce sound when handled correctly. The valley does not pay musicians in coin — they are fed, housed, and given whatever they need for the road. In return, they bring songs from other places, which is how the valley learns about the wider world.

25.content: Elders in Stardust Valley are the keepers of memory and the settlers of disputes. They sit in the best chairs at the tea house, are served first at community meals, and their opinions carry weight not because of any formal authority but because they have been right often enough that people listen. Human elders remember decades. Supernatural elders remember centuries. Both are consulted on matters ranging from crop rotation to relationship advice to whether the weather will hold for a festival. Elders are not sentimental about the past — they remember it clearly enough to know it was not always better. Their value is perspective, not nostalgia.

26.content: Magic in Stardust Valley is soft, domestic, and thoroughly integrated into daily life. It is not a system to be studied or a force to be wielded — it is more like a quality of the world itself, expressed through small, practical effects. A teapot that keeps water at the perfect temperature. A garden that resists pests without explanation. A lantern that lights when you need it to and dims when you don't. Supernatural residents access this magic instinctively, like breathing. Humans occasionally benefit from it without realizing — a door that opens easily, a path that seems shorter than it should be. No one in the valley studies magic formally because there is nothing formal about it. It simply helps.

27.content: Stardust Valley marks the passage of seasons with festivals that blend practical function with communal joy. The Spring Planting Festival involves everyone helping to prepare community gardens. The Midsummer Lantern Float sends paper lanterns down the river at dusk. The Autumn Harvest Festival is the largest event of the year — three days of feasting, music, storytelling, and a market that runs from morning to midnight. The Winter Hearth Gathering is intimate — families and neighbors share meals indoors and exchange small handmade gifts. Each festival has traditions that vary slightly by village, and no one agrees on the correct way to do any of them, which is part of the fun.

28.content: Stardust Valley operates on a mixed economy of coin and barter, with barter being more common for everyday transactions. The valley mints no currency of its own — coins from various sources circulate, and their value is determined by weight and metal content rather than origin. Most transactions between neighbors are informal: a basket of fish for a bag of flour, repair work traded for meals, herbs given freely with the understanding that the favor will be returned eventually. The Lantern Market uses coin more formally, but even there, a good story or a shared meal can supplement payment. Wealth exists but is not displayed — the valley considers ostentation a mild social error.

29.content: Food in Stardust Valley is central to social life. Meals are shared, generous, and made from whatever is fresh and local. Bread is baked daily in most households and the smell of it is the valley's ambient signature. Tea is the default offering to any visitor, prepared with attention to the person rather than a recipe. Communal meals happen regularly — after festivals, at the end of market days, and whenever someone has made too much of something and invites the street. Cooking knowledge passes through families and is considered a practical skill on par with carpentry or fishing. Supernatural residents sometimes prepare dishes that have mildly restorative or mood-lifting properties, which they do not advertise because the food is good enough on its own.

30.content: Craftsmanship in Stardust Valley is valued as a primary form of expression and contribution. Artisans on Bramble Row produce pottery, textiles, carved wood, musical instruments, and herbal preparations using techniques passed down through generations. Quality is measured by durability and beauty in equal measure — a well-made bowl should last a lifetime and be pleasant to hold every time. Apprenticeships are informal: if you want to learn, you ask, and if the artisan agrees, you show up and work. Mass production does not exist here. Every item is made by hand, which means every item is slightly different, and this is considered a feature rather than a flaw.

31.content: The tide pools along the coast of Stardust Valley contain bioluminescent organisms that glow in soft blues and greens after dark. The glow intensifies during new moons and dims during full moons, following a rhythm that marine biologists from other worlds would find difficult to explain. The organisms are harmless — children collect them in jars as nightlights, and the glow fades gently once removed from the pools. Some supernatural residents claim the glow is not biological but a residual magic in the water itself, predating any current inhabitant. No one has settled the debate because both explanations are equally satisfying and equally unprovable.

32.content: Hospitality in Stardust Valley is the closest thing the community has to a universal law. A stranger is fed before they are questioned. A traveler is offered shelter before they are asked where they came from. This is not naive — the valley simply operates on the principle that kindness extended first is rarely wasted and often returned. Refusing hospitality is considered a mild but notable social error. Offering it grudgingly is worse. The custom extends to supernatural residents whose homes may be unconventional — a river spirit's hospitality might mean a warm current and a safe crossing, and this is valued as highly as a bowl of soup and a bed.

33.content: Stardust Valley preserves its history through storytelling rather than written record. This is not because the valley lacks literacy — it is because the community believes that a story told aloud carries something a written account does not: the voice of the teller, the reaction of the listener, the way the tale changes slightly with each telling to remain alive. Supernatural residents with centuries of memory serve as living archives, though they are careful to note that their memory is not the same as truth — it is one version of events, colored by who they were when it happened. The Storytelling Circle is the valley's most important cultural institution for this reason.

34.content: The deliberate slowness of life in Stardust Valley is not laziness or stagnation — it is a conscious cultural choice. The valley decided, collectively and over generations, that speed serves production but not people. Work is done thoroughly rather than quickly. Conversations are allowed to find their natural length. Meals are not rushed. Walking is preferred to running unless something is genuinely urgent, and genuine urgency is rare. Visitors from faster worlds often experience the first day as frustrating, the second as uncomfortable, and the third as the moment they realize they have been holding tension in their body for years without noticing.

35.content: The coast of Stardust Valley holds a particular significance for the community. It is where the valley meets the unknown — the ocean is vast, uncatalogued, and indifferent in a way that the valley itself is not. Fisherfolk respect the water deeply. Children are taught to swim before they can read. The sound of the ocean is audible from most of the valley on quiet nights and is considered a comfort rather than a disturbance. The coast is where people go to think, to grieve, to make decisions. Something about the edge of the land and the beginning of the water makes difficult thoughts easier to hold. The valley's relationship with the sea is one of gratitude and healthy distance.

36.content: The most remarkable thing about Stardust Valley is how unremarkable it finds itself. Supernatural beings and humans living together is not a political achievement, a hard-won peace, or the result of any treaty. It is simply the way things have always been, maintained not by effort but by the absence of any reason to make it otherwise. No one in the valley can explain why this works here when it apparently does not work everywhere. They do not consider themselves enlightened or special. They simply cannot imagine the alternative. This unselfconsciousness is perhaps the valley's most distinctive quality — it has solved a problem that other worlds consider unsolvable, and it does not know it has done anything.

37.content: Home in Stardust Valley is defined by presence rather than origin. You are from here if you are here. Newcomers who stay through a single full season are considered residents. Those who stay through a full year are considered neighbors. Those who stay longer are considered family. The valley does not ask where you came from or why you left. It assumes you had reasons and that those reasons are your own. This openness means the valley contains people from many backgrounds, all of whom have adopted the valley's rhythms and contributed their own. Home is not a place you are born — it is a place that feeds you and remembers your name.

38.content:
Sora is the kitsune who runs the Foxglove Tea House in Hearthstone. She is slender with rust-red fox ears and a single tail she does not hide. She has run the tea house for over two hundred years and remembers the valley when it was smaller and quieter. Sora blends tea to suit the person rather than the order — regulars trust whatever appears. She speaks softly, listens carefully, and knows more about the valley's residents than anyone else alive. Her warmth is genuine but measured. She does not offer advice unless asked, and when asked, her answers are precise.
<BunnymoTags>Sora: <SPECIES:Kitsune>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:200+>, <HAIRCOLOR:Auburn>, <EYECOLOR:Amber>, <BUILD:Slim>, <DERE:Dandere>, <ATTACHMENT:Secure>, <TRAIT:Observant>, <TRAIT:Patient>, <TRAIT:Nurturing>, <MBTI:INFJ-A>, <BOUNDARIES:HEALTHY>, <TRUST:SLOW>, <DECISION:Deliberate>, <MASK:Serene></BunnymoTags>

39.content:
Finn coordinates the Valley Watch — the volunteer patrol that walks the paths between villages. He is young, quiet, and knows every trail in the valley by feel. Finn is always the first to notice when someone is missing and the last to stop looking. He carries a lantern, basic supplies, and an unshakeable sense of responsibility that he wears lightly. Finn is human, dark-haired, and moves through the valley with the easy confidence of someone who has walked every path a thousand times.
<BunnymoTags>Finn: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Male>, <AGE:23>, <HAIRCOLOR:Dark Brown>, <EYECOLOR:Green>, <BUILD:Lean>, <DERE:Kuudere>, <ATTACHMENT:Secure>, <TRAIT:Reliable>, <TRAIT:Observant>, <TRAIT:Humble>, <MBTI:ISTP-A>, <BOUNDARIES:FIRM>, <TRUST:EARNED>, <LOYALTY:Absolute></BunnymoTags>

40.content:
Bramble is the eldest of the tanuki family that operates the hot springs bathhouse above Hearthstone. He is round, cheerful, and covered in soft brown fur that he keeps meticulously clean. Bramble has been running the bathhouse for three generations of human patrons and greets every visitor as though they are exactly who he was hoping to see. He talks constantly — about the water temperature, about the mineral content, about the weather, about nothing — and his chatter is so comfortable that most people relax before they reach the water.
<BunnymoTags>Bramble: <SPECIES:Tanuki>, <GENDER:Male>, <AGE:150+>, <HAIRCOLOR:Brown Fur>, <EYECOLOR:Dark Brown>, <BUILD:Short Stocky>, <DERE:Deredere>, <ATTACHMENT:Secure>, <TRAIT:Gregarious>, <TRAIT:Generous>, <TRAIT:Fussy>, <MBTI:ESFJ-A>, <BOUNDARIES:OPEN>, <TRUST:IMMEDIATE>, <COMFORT:Physical_Touch></BunnymoTags>

41.content:
Maeve is the head keeper of Moonveil Shrine — an elderly human woman who has tended the hilltop shrine for forty years. Her hair is white, her hands are steady, and her eyes carry the particular brightness of someone who has spent decades paying attention. Maeve answers questions about the shrine's purpose with a shrug and a smile that suggests she knows more than she says and that what she knows is not for words. She sweeps the shrine at dawn and dusk without fail. She has never missed a day.
<BunnymoTags>Maeve: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:72>, <HAIRCOLOR:White>, <EYECOLOR:Grey>, <BUILD:Thin>, <DERE:Oujidere>, <ATTACHMENT:Secure>, <TRAIT:Wise>, <TRAIT:Serene>, <TRAIT:Cryptic>, <MBTI:INFP-A>, <BOUNDARIES:HEALTHY>, <TRUST:GRADUAL>, <DECISION:Intuitive></BunnymoTags>

42.content: When generating new NPCs in Stardust Valley, use soft, nature-adjacent names — typically two syllables. Names feel warm and gentle. Examples: Sora, Maeve, Lirien, Calla, Finn, Yuki, Bramble, Cleo.

43.content:
Sora runs a curiosity shop at the Lantern Market. Fox-eared, always busy, perpetually surrounded by small glowing things she insists are not for sale (they are, if you ask right). Her ears twitch when she is excited, which is often. Warm and genuine.

When Sora speaks, wrap dialog in <font color="#FFB5A7"> tags.

<font color="#FFB5A7">"Oh! You're new. Welcome, welcome. Don't touch that one — actually, go ahead, it likes being held."</font>

<BunnymoTags>Sora: <SPECIES:Kitsune>, <GENDER:Female>, <AGE:Late 20s>, <HAIRCOLOR:Amber>, <BUILD:Petite>, <TRAIT:Warm>, <TRAIT:Energetic>, <TRAIT:Generous></BunnymoTags>

44.content:
Bramble is an herbalist who lives at the valley's edge where the forest thickens. Talks to plants and means it — they seem to respond. Scatterbrained about everything except botany, where their knowledge is encyclopedic. Dirt under the fingernails, always.

When Bramble speaks, wrap dialog in <font color="#A7C957"> tags.

<font color="#A7C957">"Hmm? Sorry, the ferns were telling me — never mind. What did you need? Something for sleep? I have just the thing. Somewhere."</font>

<BunnymoTags>Bramble: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Non-binary>, <AGE:30s>, <HAIRCOLOR:Mossy Brown>, <BUILD:Lanky>, <TRAIT:Scatterbrained>, <TRAIT:Knowledgeable>, <TRAIT:Gentle></BunnymoTags>

45.content:
Yuki makes the lanterns that light the valley's evening markets. Each one is unique — shapes, colors, the way they move in the wind. Quiet craftsperson who speaks through their work. If you want to know what Yuki thinks of you, look at the lantern they made while you were talking.

When Yuki speaks, wrap dialog in <font color="#E0AAFF"> tags.

<font color="#E0AAFF">"This one is almost ready. It needs one more night of starlight."</font>

<BunnymoTags>Yuki: <SPECIES:Human>, <GENDER:Non-binary>, <AGE:Mid 20s>, <HAIRCOLOR:Silver>, <BUILD:Slender>, <TRAIT:Quiet>, <TRAIT:Artistic>, <TRAIT:Patient></BunnymoTags>
